explanation: The property states that whenever the system is in path-planning mode, it should justify its decision. In LTL, this is expressed as 'G(pathPlanningMode -> justifyDecision)'. This LTL formula captures the requirement that during all moments when pathPlanningMode is true (i.e., while the robot is path planning), the atomic proposition justifyDecision must also hold (i.e., the robot must explain its choice). The formula properly captures the obligation described in the natural language, which is conditional on path-planning mode being active. Other potential formulas, such as those requiring justification at all times (G justifyDecision), wouldn't reflect the dependency on path-planning mode specified in the prompt.

The natural language specifies that whenever the robot is path planning (i.e., whenever 'pathPlanningMode' holds), it should explain its path choice (i.e., 'justifyDecision' should occur). In LTL, this is captured using a global implication: globally, if the robot is in path planning mode, it must justify its decision at that moment. The formula G(pathPlanningMode -> justifyDecision) requires justification whenever path planning is active. This directly captures the requirement from the natural language. No prior LTL formulas were given to compare with.

This LTL formula captures the requirement that whenever the robot is in path-planning mode, it should explain (justify) its path choice. In LTL, 'Globally, if pathPlanningMode holds, then justifyDecision must also hold' is written as G(pathPlanningMode -> justifyDecision). This ensures that at all points in time, if the system is performing path planning, it must also be justifying its choice. No numerical comparisons are needed, and the atomic propositions are used as specified.

The natural language requirement states that whenever the robot is performing path planning, it should explain (justify) its path choice. In LTL, this is usually captured by saying: whenever pathPlanningMode holds, then justifyDecision should also hold at the same moment. The global operator (G) ensures this property holds throughout the execution. The implication (->) ensures that at any time path planning is happening, justification is required. No previously given LTL properties are present, so only this formalization is relevant.

The natural language requirement states that whenever the system is in path-planning mode, the robot should explain or justify its decision. In LTL, this can be captured by saying that globally, whenever pathPlanningMode holds, then at the same time justifyDecision should hold. This is encoded as G(pathPlanningMode -> justifyDecision). This ensures that at all points in time where the system is in path-planning mode, the justification for the decision is also provided. Alternative LTL formulas might involve temporal delay or waiting, but the phrasing 'when path planning' implies justification should occur simultaneously or immediately during that mode, not at some indefinite future time.

The natural language requirement states that whenever the system is in path planning mode, the robot should explain (justify) its path choice. This implies that, at all times globally, if the system enters or is in path planning mode, it must justify its decision (perhaps immediately or at the same time). The most direct LTL expression for this is 'G(pathPlanningMode -> justifyDecision)', which globally enforces that whenever 'pathPlanningMode' holds, 'justifyDecision' must also hold, matching the requirement for an explanation during all instances of path planning mode.

The property expresses that whenever the system is in path-planning mode (pathPlanningMode), it is always required that the robot justifies its decision (justifyDecision). This is captured by the formula 'G(pathPlanningMode -> justifyDecision)', meaning that globally, whenever pathPlanningMode holds, justifyDecision must also hold. This directly reflects the natural language requirement that the robot should explain its path choice whenever it is planning a path. Previous LTL properties that might simply require justification always (e.g., G(justifyDecision)) would be too strong and not capture the conditional nature related to path planning mode.

The property requires that whenever the system is in path-planning mode ('pathPlanningMode'), it must be the case that 'justifyDecision' (the robot justifies its decision) holds at the same time. This is captured by the formula 'G(pathPlanningMode -> justifyDecision)', which asserts that globally (at all times), if the system is planning the path, it provides a justification for its path choice. This formulation accurately reflects the input natural language: whenever path planning occurs, a justification must be provided. No previous LTL property was given to compare against.

The property requires that whenever the system is in path-planning mode (pathPlanningMode), the robot must eventually justify its decision (justifyDecision). Using the temporal operator G (globally), we specify that at all times, if path planning is ongoing (pathPlanningMode), it is guaranteed that at some point (F) the robot will provide an explanation (justifyDecision). This captures the requirement that during path planning, the robot should always explain its path choice. No numerical comparisons are used, and only the specified atomic propositions are referenced.

The property requires that whenever the system is operating in path-planning mode (pathPlanningMode), it must justify its decision (justifyDecision). In other words, at every point along the timeline, if the robot is in path planning mode, then it should explain (justify) its path choice at that moment. This directly captures the requirement from the natural language: 'When path planning, the robot should explain its path choice.'
